title: Barley sugar boiled sweets
subtitle: Traditional British lemon hard-crack sweets
excerpt: Traditional British boiled sweets: sugar cooked to hard crack with lemon juice and zest, then shaped into twists or pillows during the brief window while still pliable. They set clear amber and hard. About 40 sweets.
sourceNotes: Barley sugar boiled sweets are documented in British confectionery from the seventeenth century; the name derives from an older form made with barley water, though by the nineteenth century most recipes used plain sugar with lemon and acid. Pre-1928 recipes appear in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), and in pre-1928 British household confectionery manuals. The modern lemon-flavoured form without actual barley is attested in pre-1928 household guides and domestic science texts available through the British Library.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin with greaseproof paper between layers for 2 weeks. Do not refrigerate; condensation will make the sweets sticky.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Stovetop only. Target: 150 to 155°C (hard crack stage). A sugar thermometer is essential. Shaping must happen quickly once the sugar is poured; have all equipment ready before cooking starts.

glossaryTerms.definition: Boiled sugar cooked to the hard crack stage (150 to 155°C) is glass-like when cold but briefly pliable as it cools on the tray. This shaping window lasts only 2 to 3 minutes: too soon and the sugar flows; too late and it snaps. Work quickly and have your oiled scissors or knife ready before you pour. If the sugar hardens before shaping is complete, it can be returned to the oven at a very low temperature to soften again. The finished sweets are hard and transparent; any cloudiness indicates underdone sugar or moisture absorbed during cooling.

content.text: Barley sugar sweets are cooked to hard crack and shaped during the

before the sugar hardens. The finished sweets are clear, hard, and lemon-flavoured. The name comes from an older form made with barley water; this version is the modern household recipe. About 40 sweets.

Oil a large baking tray and have oiled scissors and a palette knife ready. Put the sugar, water, lemon juice, and cream of tartar in a heavy-based saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar is fully dissolved, then stop stirring. Increase the heat to medium-high and cook to 152°C. Remove from the heat, add the lemon zest, and swirl once to combine. Pour onto the oiled tray in a thin, even layer.

Leave the poured sugar for 2 to 3 minutes until the edges are just firm but the centre is still glossy and pliable. Working from the edges inward, use the oiled palette knife to fold the edges into the centre. Use oiled scissors to snip into small pieces, or twist portions into short barley-sugar twists. Work as fast as possible; if the sugar sets before you finish, place the tray in an oven at 100°C for 2 minutes to soften. Transfer the shaped sweets to a sheet of greaseproof paper and leave to cool and harden completely, about 30 minutes.
